EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long-lived EF1 tornado tracked west to east across Flagler county during the pre-dawn hours. The tornado entered the west side of the county near Mud Lake within the Haw Creek Preserve around 5:15 am and continued a steady east to northeast track across the central part of the county and impacted Korona and then exited offshore of the local coast near Gamble Rogers Memorial State Recreation Area, about 1 mile south of Flagler Beach around 5:40 am.  The storm survey revealed the most substantial damage within the Korona city limits where EF1 damage occurred. Numerous trees, powerlines and structures were damaged. One masonary structure had its roof blown off in Korona.  The following are timing of reports to relayed to the NWS from Flagler County EM: 530 am 2 SSW Flagler Beach a tree was blown down through a roof of a home along Creek Bluff Run in Bulowville, 532 am many roads were closed near Korona and County Road 330 due to widespread tree damage, 532 am several structures had damage behind the White Eagle Lounge in Korona, 536 am a motorhome was flipped and had roof and deck damage along South 28th Street in Flagler Beach, 536 am oak trunks were twisted and trees heavily damaged along Creek Bluff Run, 540 am debris was across the roads and street lights were damaged between South 23rd Street and South 28th Street in South Flagler Beach, and at 540 am some structural damage was reported between south Flagler Beach and Bulowville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Dorian moved slowly northwest away from Grand Bahama Island late in the evening September 2. Dorian tracked parallel to the eastern Florida coast over the next 36-48 hours at Category 2-3 strength, making its closest approach when the center was approximately 80 miles east of the tip of Cape Canaveral. Rain bands on the western periphery of Dorian's circulation moved very near the east central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds in coastal areas of Brevard and Volusia counties, and gusts to tropical storm force along the Treasure Coast. As the center of Hurricane Dorian moved north of the Cape, a rain band developed on the western side of the hurricane, and gusts to tropical storm force were observed in Orange and Seminole Counties. There were no direct hurricane-related fatalities, however 5 indirect fatalities occurred during the preparedness and evacuation phases of the hurricane. Preliminary damage estimates across east central Florida exceeded $32 million, primarily related to beach erosion.||A storm surge of 2-3 feet AGL affected areas north of Cape Canaveral, and storm surge of near 2 feet AGL impacted areas south of Cape Canaveral through Martin County. Moderate to major beach erosion occurred along many sections of the east-central Florida coastline. Water levels rises within the coastal lagoon system reached between 1 and 2 feet above normal levels due to a combination of coastal surge, persistent north winds, and reduced drainage through the inlets to the Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Indian River County, tropical storm force sustained winds affected the barrier island, with gusts to tropical storm force in squalls through much of the remainder of the county. The highest sustained wind was measured at Sebastian Inlet Pier (48 mph from the northeast at 1721LST on September 3) and the peak gust was also at Sebastian Inlet Pier (61 mph from the northeast at 1821LST on September 3). Across the county, 781 people evacuated into storm shelters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bow echo moved across extreme coastal Alabama into the western Florida Panhandle on the morning of May 12th. The bow echo produced significant wind damage in coastal AL with some wind damage extending into the western Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts downed trees and tree limbs on Mers Lane.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Dorian moved northwest parallel to the east central Florida coast, the Martin County beaches experienced varying degrees of beach erosion, with moderate to major beach erosion in some areas, due to an estimated 2 foot AGL storm surge. The strong onshore winds and large swells over two high tide cycles contributed to the surge. Similar water level rises occurred within the Indian River Lagoon system where shoreside flooding occurred in some locations, including at Sandspirit Park and along the east side of the Saint Lucie River in Stuart. The economic loss due to beach erosion was estimated at $10.5 million due primarily to beach erosion. There was significant beach erosion north of Saint Lucie Inlet, with approximately 40 feet of beach lost. Although there were no dunes breached, ocean water did intrude between some homes on MacArthur Boulevard in South Hutchinson Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northwesterly winds behind a cold front lead to numerous strong rip currents along the Gulf beaches of Collier County. Several people were rescued from rough surf and rip currents, receiving minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Collier Fire Rescue rescued 1 adult and five children for a total of six people from rough surf and rip currents during the morning hours at Wiggins Pass State Park. The victims were transported by Collier County EMS to local hospitals and later released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high pressure located over the central Atlantic was the dominant feature affecting weather over central Florida. This led to west to southwest winds at the surface that pushed deep moisture from the Gulf across the area. Hot daytime temperatures, and cold temperatures in the middle atmosphere provided the necessary ingredients for strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. These storms developed along the sea breezes and outflow boundaries that were produced. One thunderstorm became severe in southwestern Volusia County where it produced hail and damaging winds. Another storm became severe in Brevard County and produced a severe wind gust along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spectrum News 13 relayed report of penny to quarter-sized hail near the intersection of Interstate 4 and Saxton Boulevard in Deltona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that moved into South Florida during the afternoon hours interacted with the east coast sea breeze across Palm Beach County, leading to strong to severe storms developing across the metropolitan areas. Several reports of hail and wind damage were received in the central and northern portions of the Palm Beach County metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Beach Gardens PD reported multiple power lines down along PGA Boulevard and Military Trail. Hail up to 0.50 was also reported in the vicinity. Time based on radar wind signature of over 60 mph about 300 feet above the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms moved through South Florida during the afternoon and evening hours ahead of an approaching cold front. The storms that developed ahead of the boundary produced strong wind gusts across the region, as well as an area of wind damage across Key Biscayne in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous broken tree branches at Bill Baggs State Park on the southern tip of Key Biscayne. Many branches were several inches thick. Wind estimated at 60 mph based on level of damage as well as measured wind gust of 56 mph around the same time at WxFlow Biscayne Bay mesonet site XKBS about 2 miles to the southwest. Time is reasonably estimated based on radar data and the time of the Biscayne Bay observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Dorian passed between 80-100 NM offshore of the local Atlantic Coast through the day on Sept. 4, 2019, as a Cat 2 storm on its closet approach. Ahead of Dorian, elevated King Tides and persistent onshore flow already raised water levels within the local estuaries by about 1-1.5 ft including the ICWW and St. Johns River basin. Total water rise (storm tide) associated with Dorian was up to 3.2 ft MHHW along the St. Johns county coast to about 1.5-2 ft MHHW within the St. Johns River basin.  Heaviest rainfall was confined to a couple bands in St. Johns & Flagler counties. Wind damage was confined to the immediate coast and areas generally east of the I-95 corridor. There was damage to some beach structures including piers and boardwalks. Some homes flooded in Davis Shores of St. Augustine due to surge flooding & rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dorian impacts to the county were mainly confined to the immediate beachfront. Scattered trees and large limbs were blown down, and some of this tree debris blocked roads. Most of the tree damage was confined to Arlington and the beaches communities. Storm tide flooding was a larger impact than wind damage and produced flooding and damage to the immediate coast with most damage to Huguenot Park where the boardwalk was majorly damaged. Part of the boardwalk was broken off by wave action and deposited about 100 yards up the beach. The access road to Huguenot Park was completely flooded by storm surge flooding. The Jacksonville Beach Pier sustained damage but the extend was not yet determined. Sand loss occurred to the berm along the entire coast. Farther inland along the St. Johns River in downtown Jacksonville, storm surge flooded the immediate River Walk and overflowed Hogans Creek. At this time structure flooding was unknown. At 1147 am on 9/4, the media reported photos of a large tree that was blown down onto cars in the Lakeshore neighborhood near Ortega. At 2 pm, the storm surge value reached 2.13 ft MHHW at the Mayport tide gauge. Coastal flooding begins at 1.8 ft MHHW at this location. This storm surge value was the highest recorded since the 2.8 ft value measured during Hurricane Irma and the 2.13 ft MHHW value tide for the 10th highest storm surge value measured at this location. Peak winds included 32 mph (gusts 51 mph) at NAS Mayport , 32 mph (gusts 43 mph) at Jacksonville International Airport, 30 mph (gusts to 45 mph) at Cecil Field on the JAX Westside and 36 mph (gusts 48 mph) at NAS Jacksonville on the St. Johns River. Other observations from the WeatherFlow mesonet included 41 mph (gusts 54 mph) at Huguenot Park, 44 mph (gusts 55 mph) at the Jacksonville Beach Pier, 43 mph (gusts 55 mph) at Buck Island west of Mayport in the St. Johns River, and 28 mph (gusts 41 mph) at the Terminal Channel in downtown Jacksonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system was moving across the northeastern Gulf of Mexico and the Florida peninsula. Ahead of this system winds across central Florida were from the southwest, bringing in plenty of deep moisture from the Gulf. Strong daytime heating, combined with the presence of the approaching storm system led to the development of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across east central Florida in the afternoon. Severe thunderstorms impacted the Orlando Metro area, as well as Lake, Volusia, Brevard, and St. Lucie counties. Many reports of downed trees and power lines, damage to roofs and other weak structures were received. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were measured at the Kennedy Space Center in Brevard County, and hail was reported in Orange and Seminole Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officials at the Port Mayaca Lock and Dam reported seeing several large branches on US Highway 441 between Port Mayaca and J and S Fish Camp. Also, the public reported a tree fell and damaged 2 cars at J and S Fish Camp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system was moving across the northeastern Gulf of Mexico and the Florida peninsula. Ahead of this system winds across central Florida were from the southwest, bringing in plenty of deep moisture from the Gulf. Strong daytime heating, combined with the presence of the approaching storm system led to the development of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across east central Florida in the afternoon. Severe thunderstorms impacted the Orlando Metro area, as well as Lake, Volusia, Brevard, and St. Lucie counties. Many reports of downed trees and power lines, damage to roofs and other weak structures were received. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were measured at the Kennedy Space Center in Brevard County, and hail was reported in Orange and Seminole Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed reports of quarter-sized hail in Union Park near the intersection of Antibes Court and Chickasaw Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Dorian passed between 80-100 NM offshore of the local Atlantic Coast through the day on Sept. 4, 2019, as a Cat 2 storm on its closet approach. Ahead of Dorian, elevated King Tides and persistent onshore flow already raised water levels within the local estuaries by about 1-1.5 ft including the ICWW and St. Johns River basin. Total water rise (storm tide) associated with Dorian was up to 3.2 ft MHHW along the St. Johns county coast to about 1.5-2 ft MHHW within the St. Johns River basin.  Heaviest rainfall was confined to a couple bands in St. Johns & Flagler counties. Wind damage was confined to the immediate coast and areas generally east of the I-95 corridor. There was damage to some beach structures including piers and boardwalks. Some homes flooded in Davis Shores of St. Augustine due to surge flooding & rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 115 am on 9/4, the St. Augustine Pier reported a sustained wind of 44 mph with a gust to 52 mph. At 155 pm, a storm surge value of 3.16 ft MHHW datum was measured at the temporarily deployed USGS Tidal Gauge along the Tolomoto River near St. Augustine. Coastal flooding for this location begins around 1.6 ft MHHW. At 3 pm, the water level reached 2.52 ft MHHW on the Tolomoto River north of St. Augustine airport via a DEP Tide Gauge. Coast flooding occurs around 1.2 ft MHHW at this location. This was the highest sea levle rise since 3.61 ft MHHW during Hurricane Irma in 2017.  There were some homes flooded in the Davis Shores neighborhood near St. Augustine. A new cut in the beach was formed near Summerhaven. There was overwash and beach erosion observed in Vilano Beach. Trees and branches were blown down within the St. Augustine city limits. The St. Augustine C-Man station recorded a peak sustained wind of 51 mph on 9/4 at 9 am with a peak gust of 62 mph on 9/4 at 8:58 am.  The Weatherflow mesonet sensor at Cresent Beach Summerhouse measured a peak wind of 39 mph on 9/4 at 6:48 am and a peak gust of 58 mph on 9/4 at 108 am. The peak wind measured at the St. Augustine Airport was 38 mph on 9/4 at 1:46 am with a peak gust of 47 mph on 9/4 at 1:45 am. Storm total rainfall of 3.47 inches was measured about 6 miles SSW of Palm Valley and 3.31 inches about 2 miles S of Sawgrass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm came onshore in Volusia County, and quickly became severe over Daytona Beach. Observations of 50 knot or greater winds occurred at three separate, but close by locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Daytona Beach International Airport measured a peak wind gust of 53 knots from the east-southeast. Also, a two mesonet stations, EW4959 and FW3022, located nearby at the Daytona International Speedway measured peak wind gusts of 54 knots and 63 knots, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 69-year-old man was pulled seaward by a rip current and was unconscious when a good Samaritan and life guard pulled him from the water. Life safety measures were administered, but the man died from drowning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 0930 LST, a good Samaritan and life guard pulled an unconscious 69-year-old man from the water after he was pulled seaward by a rip current. The life guard administered CPR on the man in order to revive him, but attempts were unsuccessful. The man died due to drowning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4 am on 9/4/19, emergency management reported that the Hammock Dunes Bridge weather system measured a sustained wind of 39 mph. At 7:54 am, emergency management reported power lines were blown down on Annette Street in Flagler Beach. At 9:20 am, a HAM radio operator reported a wire down, laying across Wellstream Lane northeast of Bunnell. At 2:15 pm, a residential deck collapsed and a fence was damaged in North Ocean Shore. Storm total rainfall across the county ranged from 3 - 6 inches, with the higher amounts 5-6 miles SSW of Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 60-year-old man from Sanford, Florida drowned at Daytona Beach due to rough surf conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volusia County Beach Safety Ocean Rescue reported that a 60-year-old man from Sanford, Florida drowned at Daytona Beach near the boardwalk due to high surf after he waded into the water at an unguarded beach. Rip currents were also present at the beach when the incident occurred, but high surf was the greater hazard. Bystanders reported the man started struggling in the high waves after he was chest-deep in the water. Lifeguards responded to the scene after receiving a 9-1-1 call and after a brief search pulled the man unresponsive from the water. CPR was performed on the man and he was transported to a local hospital, where he died from his injuries shortly after 1800 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man from Kissimmee, Florida drowned at Cocoa Beach after being caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed the report that a 29-year-old man from Kissimmee, Florida died when he drowned at Cocoa Beach as a result of being caught in a rip current. Around 1315 LST the man went missing near Minutemen Causeway and North Atlantic Avenue in Cocoa Beach. The man's family reported that he and his brother were swimming in the ocean when the victim was caught in a rip current and was carried further out to sea. The brother tried without success to reach him, and when the brother got back to shore the family called first responders. Beach safety and other officials searched unsuccessfully for the missing man. The next afternoon, September 24, a beachgoer noticed a body floating in the water near 11th Street South, and called police and ocean rescue officials. Officials confirmed the body was the missing man from the previous day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow around TS Humberto created squally conditions and very dangerous surf. An extremely hazardous surf and rip current day made it extremely hard for Ocean Rescue to extract a male caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Johns County Ocean Rescue reported a make in his mid 20s drowned due to rip currents. Stated that seasoned lifeguards had trouble in the hazardous waters on that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow around TS Humberto created squally conditions and very dangerous surf. An extremely hazardous surf and rip current day made it extremely hard for Ocean Rescue to extract a male caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large branch was blown over Terrell Road. The time was based on radar. Gusts across the county ranged between 40-45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that moved into South Florida during the afternoon hours interacted with the east coast sea breeze across Palm Beach County, leading to strong to severe storms developing across the metropolitan areas. Several reports of hail and wind damage were received in the central and northern portions of the Palm Beach County metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture received via social media showed large tree down on cars in a parking lot at Palm Beach State College in Palm Beach Gardens. Other pictures received of broken large tree branches in nearby North Palm Beach. Time estimated and based on radar wind signature of at least 60 mph at about 300 feet above the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing south southeasterly winds lead to a moderate risk of rip currents along the east coast beaches of South Florida. A woman was pushed by strong waves into a rip current near Jupiter in Palm Beach County, was rescued, but later died from the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 year old woman visiting from Virginia was pushed by strong waves and caught in a strong rip current off the Palm Beach County coast near Jupiter, approximately near the 3000 block of South State Highway A1A. Attempts to rescue her were made by her husband, and a bystander was able to pull her to shore. She was taken to Jupiter Medical Center but died later that evening.��Time is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Dorian moved slowly northwest away from Grand Bahama Island late in the evening September 2. Dorian tracked parallel to the eastern Florida coast over the next 36-48 hours at Category 2-3 strength, making its closest approach when the center was approximately 80 miles east of the tip of Cape Canaveral. Rain bands on the western periphery of Dorian's circulation moved very near the east central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds in coastal areas of Brevard and Volusia counties, and gusts to tropical storm force along the Treasure Coast. As the center of Hurricane Dorian moved north of the Cape, a rain band developed on the western side of the hurricane, and gusts to tropical storm force were observed in Orange and Seminole Counties. There were no direct hurricane-related fatalities, however 5 indirect fatalities occurred during the preparedness and evacuation phases of the hurricane. Preliminary damage estimates across east central Florida exceeded $32 million, primarily related to beach erosion.||A storm surge of 2-3 feet AGL affected areas north of Cape Canaveral, and storm surge of near 2 feet AGL impacted areas south of Cape Canaveral through Martin County. Moderate to major beach erosion occurred along many sections of the east-central Florida coastline. Water levels rises within the coastal lagoon system reached between 1 and 2 feet above normal levels due to a combination of coastal surge, persistent north winds, and reduced drainage through the inlets to the Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Brevard County, tropical storm force sustained winds affected the barrier islands, with gusts to tropical storm force in squalls through much of the remainder of the county on September 3 and 4 (beginning around 0800LST on September 3). The highest sustained wind was measured at Melbourne Beach (48 mph from the northeast at 1720LST on September 3) and the peak gust was at US Air Force Wind Tower 313 in Cape Canaveral (71 mph from the north-northwest at 0200LST on September 4). Approximately 1600 people evacuated into shelters. An indirect hurricane-related fatality occurred on September 1, during the preparedness phase of the event, around 1510LST when a 68-year-old man, who was putting plywood on the windows of his home, lost his balance upon a ladder and fell three stories to his death.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Orange County, wind gusts to minimal tropical storm force occurred in squalls throughout much of the county from approximately 1800LST September 3 to 1100LST September 4. However, there were three indirect hurricane-related fatalities that occurred on September 2 during the evacuation and preparedness phases of the storm. A 72-year-old man who evacuated to a hotel, collapsed while moving luggage and later died at a hospital. At 1553LST, a 62-year-old man who evacuated to a resort, went into cardiac arrest while moving luggage and was pronounced dead at a local hospital. Around 1730LST, a 56-year-old man fell from a tree while cutting limbs prior to the weather event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front located across north central Florida moved slowly south into east-central Florida during the day. Increasing wind shear, strong daytime heating, ample moisture and cold temperatures in the middle atmosphere led to the development of numerous strong thunderstorms across the Florida peninsula. West to southwest winds pushed these storms towards the east coast. One thunderstorm developed in northeastern Palm Beach County and quickly became severe as it crossed into southeastern Martin County. There the storm produced wind damage, mainly at Jonathan Dickinson Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park Ranger at Jonathan Dickinson State Park reported one pine tree uprooted, another pine tree snapped near the bottom towards the base, and several large branches down along Park Road from the railroad tracks to near the North Fork Loxahatchee River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Atlantic high pressure ridge located south of Florida resulted in southwest winds across central Florida. This pushed deep moisture over the area, and when combined with daytime heating and cold temperatures in the middle atmosphere provided the conditions for showers and thunderstorms to develop. One thunderstorm in eastern Volusia County interacted with the sea breeze front. This storm quickly became severe as it moved very slowly southward over New Smyrna Beach and Edgewater and several reports of hail were received in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of quarter sized hail in Oliver Estates in New Smyrna Beach.

Sustained winds in Martin County remained below tropical storm force as the center of Dorian remained far away to the east. Approximately 80 residents lost power for a short period, and 1271 people evacuated into shelters.

An indirect hurricane-related fatality occurred on September 4 at 0627LST when an 86-year-old man collapsed at a hurricane evacuation shelter in Kissimmee and was taken to a hospital, where he died.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Seminole County, wind gusts to minimal tropical storm force occurred in squalls through much of the county. Across the county, 237 people checked into shelters, and minor flooding occurred along the Saint Johns River near Geneva.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a severe storm moved off Lake Apopka it produced wind damage along an adjacent road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported wind damage along Walker Road in Apopka just along the eastern edge of Lake Apopka. Three palm trees were knocked over, one fell and damaged the roof of a home, and another downed nearby power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area with showers and thunderstorms. There were several reports of trees and power lines down, and one storm produced an EF2 tornado that tracked from Irwin into Ben Hill county in Georgia.  Additional EF0 tornadoes touched down in Early county, GA, Baker county, GA, and Houston county, AL.  An EF1 tornado occurred in Miller county, GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down, including on Highway 69A between Parrish Lake Road and John Redd Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered just off the northeastern U.S. coast continued to slide further into the Atlantic, allowing for a moist southeast wind flow across South Florida. This surge of moisture led to a line of showers settling across coastal and metro Palm Beach County which produced heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in northeastern Palm Beach County led to 4 to 6 inches of rain in approximately 6 hours. This rainfall led to street flooding and standing water over much of this area, including water flowing over curbs near the corner of West Indiantown Road and Maplewood Drive in Jupiter, street flooding at the corner of Ardley Road and Tudor Road in Juno Beach, at least one foot of moving water on roads near the intersection of Frederick Small Road and Military Trail, and water close to encroaching on structures in Riviera Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore flow, increasing swells, and rough surf brought hazardous marine and beach conditions to South Florida Atlantic coast beaches. At a beach in Palm Beach County, a man had to be rescued and sent to the hospital after becoming caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards rescued a swimmer caught in a rip current at Riviera Beach in the early afternoon hours (time is estimated to be around 2 PM EST). Paramedics transported the man to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area with showers and thunderstorms. There were several reports of trees and power lines down, and one storm produced an EF2 tornado that tracked from Irwin into Ben Hill county in Georgia.  Additional EF0 tornadoes touched down in Early county, GA, Baker county, GA, and Houston county, AL.  An EF1 tornado occurred in Miller county, GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five trees and two power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore flow, increasing swells, and rough surf brought hazardous marine and beach conditions to South Florida Atlantic coast beaches. At a beach in Miami, a seven people were rescued by lifeguards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards rescued 7 swimmers caught in a rip current at Miami Beach around 0137 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region behind a deep, low pressure system that developed over the Gulf and pushed eastward. Ahead of the cold front, a strong line of storms crossed through South Florida and produced heavy rain and flooding across the east coast metro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 5 inches fell across the area, resulting in stranded cars along NE 11th street and Biscayne Blvd in Miami.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region behind a deep, low pressure system that developed over the Gulf and pushed eastward. Ahead of the cold front, a strong line of storms crossed through South Florida and produced heavy rain and flooding across the east coast metro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of extensive flooding in NE Miami-Dade County from the county line at Aventura south to the North Miami area. Multiple reports received of significant street and parking lot flooding, with reports of flooded parking garages and stalled vehicles, particularly in Aventura as well as along Biscayne Boulevard and NE 123 Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, 13 December 2019, a warm front lifted and lingered across northeast Florida with surface dew points rising into the mid to upper 60s generally along and south of a line from St. Augustine to Gainesville. A strong upper level trough deepened across the central Gulf Coast region with a resultant surface low positioned over the northeast Gulf of Mexico coast just south of Panama City that evening around 10 pm EST. As the upper level trough axis started to lift and becoming positively tilted overnight into 14 December, the surface low edge farther east northeast and tracked over southeast Georgia then offshore of the local Atlantic coast through 4 am EST. The corridor of unstable low-level air remained across our southern Florida zones with surface-based CAPE values rising into the 1000-1300 J/kg range per LAPS analysis while aloft strong directional (0-6 km) bulk shear near the mid/upper level trough axis increased to 50-70 kts. The phased low-level instability, plentiful deep layer moisture and strong dynamical lift under the upper low and just south of the surface low spawned a couple of persistent supercells with long-lived mesocyclones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado appeared to have touched down shortly after 5 am and tracked ENE from near the SW corner of Saint Ambrose Church Road and County Road 305 and ended just east of County Road 207. A trained storm spotter reported a tornado  on the ground near State Road 207 and County Road 305 in Elkton around 5:05 am. Tree debris, shingles and part of a tin roof were reported in trees and across the ground.  A kitchen and bath store in Elkton sustained major roof damage as well as damage to their supplies stored outdoors including solid surface countertops. The official NWS Storm Survey estimated peak winds around 85 mph (EF0) and reported road signs that were bent and/or twisted in Elkton. Metal roof panels were stripped off of a building and thrown over a fence. A door was also extracted from a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region behind a deep, low pressure system that developed over the Gulf and pushed eastward. Ahead of the cold front, a strong line of storms crossed through South Florida and produced heavy rain and flooding across the east coast metro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FLL - Fort Lauderdale/Hollywood international airport closed due to flash flooding from 340 am through 0635 pm due to heavy rain of 6 to 9 inches that fell over the terminals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region behind a deep, low pressure system that developed over the Gulf and pushed eastward. Ahead of the cold front, a strong line of storms crossed through South Florida and produced heavy rain and flooding across the east coast metro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous unofficial weather observing stations reported rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches that caused flash flooding. Law enforcement reported that areas east of I-95 on Hollywood Blvd were completely flooded, numerous cars stranded, and that Hollywood fire department had conducted multiple rescues as water entered homes in those areas. Gulfstream Park in Hallandale Beach was under water for several hours, as well as neighborhood streets in Hallandale Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region behind a deep, low pressure system that developed over the Gulf and pushed eastward. Ahead of the cold front, a strong line of storms crossed through South Florida and produced heavy rain and flooding across the east coast metro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the general public reported that two cars were stranded due to flooding at the intersection of Andrews Blvd and Broward Blvd in Fort Lauderdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seasonally high King Tides combined with strong onshore flow produced elevated tides generally in the Minor flood range during daily high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the top of canal walls in Palm Coast and spread over grassy areas with morning high tide. Structures were not threatened by flood water. The tide gage at Mantanzas River near Bings Landing reported a high tide of 2 ft MHHW water datum around 10 am. Moderate flooding begins at 1.8 ft MHHW at this gage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate coastal flooding occurred due to the combined of seasonally high King Tides and strong onshore flow around a local Nor'Easter that was offshore of the SE Atlantic coast. A high risk of rip currents with a high surf for the NE FL coast was in effect with breakers of up to 7 feet mainly from St. Augustine southward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported minor inundation at the intersection of State Road 16 and Lewis Speedway along the San Sebastian River during high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds behind a cold front were strong enough to bring down tree branches. One of these tree branches fell on a woman injuring her while she was out for a walk in Boca Raton. The woman later died from these injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple media outlets reported that a woman was injured after a tree branch fell on her during a morning walk. There were gusty winds around 35 mph at the time behind a cold front passage. The woman died 4 days later as a result of the injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high November astronomical tides in combination with the breezy northeasterly winds brought minor coastal flooding along the east coast of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported minor coastal flooding associated with higher than normal tides today at Miami beach. Ocean water breached the sea wall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water on roadways, drives, and parking lots along the intracoastal waterway in Hollywood and Hallandale beaches. Multiple lanes of A1A with standing water of less than 1 foot deep in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced a waterspout just offshore of Stuart Beach. As the storm moved inland the waterspout came onshore and became a short-lived, weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Martin County Beach Patrol reported a waterspout offshore of Stuart Beach at 1620 LST. The beach was evacuated as the waterspout came onshore and became a short-lived tornado. Numerous reports were received from trained spotters and the public as the waterspout moved slightly inland. After moving onshore, a trained spotter relayed that the spout dissipated quickly near the Hutchinson Island Marriott at 1623 LST. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light south to southwesterly flow across the region with abundant tropical moisture allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop over the interior. Theses storms moved  into the east coast metro. With the slow storm motion heavy rainfall produced flooding in Lauderhill. One of the strongest storms produced gusty winds bringing down tree limbs and causing damage in Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports and pictures via CBS 4 WFOR of the Swap Shop flooded with a few inches of water throughout the store.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Beach Zoo staff reported several downed trees and large branches that fell into and onto animal enclosures and buildings. These damaged some surrounding fencing of some exhibits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture and southwesterly winds allowed afternoon convection to move into the east coast metro region. A line of storms moved through Broward and Palm Beach producing severe winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong line of thunderstorms produced a 58 mph wind gust measured by the WeatherBug station HLLBS located at BSO Alligator Alley west of Weston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds across the region with daily afternoon thunderstorms developing along outflow and sea breeze collisions. Storms concentrated over the interior before moving towards the west coast in the late afternoon and evening. One of the stronger storms produced hail in Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter called to report hail driving across Tamiami trail around 6PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms were able to develop along the sea breezes along both coast in the afternoon. Environmental parameters were favorable for increased instability with stronger down burst wind and hail potential across the region. Some of the strongest storms brought hail, flooding and dangerous lightning to portions of Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported dime to penny size hail at Aventura Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small brush/wildfire developed in the Tamiami Pinelands Park area in the afternoon. The fire quickly spread causing damage to two vehicles. Smoke produced very low to no visibility in the area at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple media reports of the a wildfire in Tamiami Pinelands Park. The fire quickly spread causing damage to 3 cars in the adjacent area to the park. The total acreage of the fire is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Powerful Hurricane Dorian passed 100 miles to the east of Palm Beach County over the northwestern Bahamas. As Dorian tracked very slowly through Abaco and Grand Bahama islands, outer rain bands produced wind gusts of tropical storm force over Southeast Florida, with sustained tropical storm force winds along parts of coastal Palm Beach County. A peak storm surge of 1-2 feet was observed along portions of the Atlantic coast particularly during high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerful Hurricane Dorian passed 100 miles to the east of Palm Beach County over the northwestern Bahamas. ||As Dorian tracked very slowly through Abaco and Grand Bahama islands, outer rain bands produced wind gusts of tropical storm force over Southeast Florida, with sustained tropical storm force winds along parts of coastal Palm Beach County.  Maximum sustained winds were generally between 35-50 mph along coastal Palm Beach County, with the highest sustained reading of 49 mph from the north recorded at a WxFlow mesonet station XJUP at Juno Beach at 11:59 PM on September 2nd. Tropical storm force wind gusts were measured as high as 50-60 mph. A total of around 2,250 customers lost power in Palm Beach County. Overall wind impacts were minor.||Lowest recorded sea level pressure in South Florida was 999.9 mb at Juno Beach Pier at 3:49 PM on September 3rd.||A peak storm surge of 1-2 feet was observed along portions of the Atlantic coast particularly during high tide, causing overtopping of seawalls and resulting in flooding of waterfront streets, marinas, docks, and piers. A few dockside structures had minor water intrusion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the new moon phase caused coastal flooding along portions of the east coast. This flooding was related to salt water inundation with sea water over-topping sea walls, docks, boat ramps and coming up out of storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal flooding observed near the high tide in the following locations: NE Miami at the intersection of NE 10 Avenue and NE 81 Street with water as much as 8-10 inches deep in parts of the intersection; Matheson Hammock Park with as much as 4-6 inches of water covering the parking lot; and Brickell Bay Drive and SE 12 Street with water a few inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore flow and rough surfed brought hazardous marine and beach conditions to South Florida Atlantic coast beaches. At a beach in Palm Beach a teenager had to be rescued and sent to the hospital after becoming caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards rescued a teenage swimmer caught in a rip current at Lake Worth Beach around 615 PM EDT. Paramedics transported the teen to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the new moon phase caused coastal flooding along portions of the east coast. This flooding was related to salt water inundation with sea water over-topping sea walls, docks, boat ramps and coming up out of storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported minor coastal flooding around the time of high tide on low-lying coastal roads near State Road A1A in Broward County this morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the new moon phase caused coastal flooding along portions of the east coast. This flooding was related to salt water inundation with sea water over-topping sea walls, docks, boat ramps and coming up out of storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of coastal flooding during high tide across coastal Miami-Dade County. These include intersections at different neighborhoods, Brickell, Matheson Hammock, and Miami Beach. A few inches to up to a foot of standing water was observed across Coastal Miami-Dade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad Atlantic ridge axis south of FL creeps northward, though steering flow remains westerly, so numerous storms and thunderstorms across the region, specifically the interior and Palm Beach County due to the aforementioned flow and diurnal heating. Drier air aloft supports stronger storms. One of the storms produced lightning that struck a man walking his dog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reports a man was critically injured due to a lightning strike. He was injured while walking his dog in Lake Park. The man was transported to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with sufficient boundary layer moisture and sea breeze development supports scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across most of South Florida, especially during the afternoon. Unidirectional northwesterly low to mid level steering flow helps to push the majority of convection towards the interior and east coast. With the development of the Atlantic sea breeze, much of the convection focused over Broward and Miami-Dade Counties, where some showers/storms produced locally heavy rainfall, and thus flooding, gusty winds, and a waterspout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture received by a WFO NWS Miami employee via social media of flooding (roughly 1 ft deep) near the intersection of NW 37th Ave and NW 24th St near Miami International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary upper level troughing over the region helped keep deep moisture in place to produce widespread convection. Strong thunderstorms developed along the pinned east coast sea breeze. One thunderstorm produced a wind gust that knocked down a power line in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coral springs/Parkland Fire Department reported via twitter that Riverside Drive was closed in both directions at Cardinal Road due to a power line down on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture in place allowed for widespread rainfall over South Florida. Modest southwesterly flow helped thunderstorms along the Gulf breeze to push across the peninsula. Heavy rainfall and lightning was produced as these storms collided with the east coast sea breeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported heavy rainfall of 2 to 3.5 inches which caused street flooding near the Intersection of 56th Ave and Sheridan street around Sheridan plaza, between 1 pm and 4 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Established easterly flow, combined with plentiful low-level moisture, allowed for scattered showers and a few thunderstorms to develop over the Atlantic waters during the morning. These showers/storms moved onshore along the east coast metro during the morning hours. Heavy rainfall rates lead to flooding of streets across areas of Miami-Dade Counties, especially with antecedent soil conditions from prior days rainfall. High tides also contributed to minor coastal flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSVN meteorologist reported street  flooding near Little Havana by Flagler Street. Water halfway up some cars parked by the median.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture in place allowed for widespread rainfall over South Florida. Modest southwesterly flow helped thunderstorms along the Gulf breeze to push across the peninsula. Heavy rainfall and lightning was produced as these storms collided with the east coast sea breeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media from WPEC reported a 38-year-old man was under a tree in a wheelchair next to his home when he struck by lightning near the 2700 block of West Golf Road in Pompano Beach. He was taken to a nearby hospital in critical condition, but survived his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer moisture in place, along with a remnant upper level trough helped produce scattered to numerous storms across South Florida. These storms produced gusty winds and heavy rainfall that lead to flash flooding along the east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 54 mph/ 47 knots from the west was measured at the Wx flow station XMGN in Mangonia Park. Sensor is 74 feet above ground level. This wind gust snapped a tree snapped at the Hampton Court Apartments in Mangonia Park, reported via the Snapchat map.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer moisture in place, along with a remnant upper level trough helped produce scattered to numerous storms across South Florida. These storms produced gusty winds and heavy rainfall that lead to flash flooding along the east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports and videos from WPTV of flooding due to heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches falling within 30 minutes across portions of West Palm Beach. This heavy rainfall caused streets to become impassable with some cars stuck in deep water, which reached up to the headlights. Streets which had significant flooding include: Flagler Drive, Parker Avenue, and Banyan Street east of Tamarind Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Palm Beach Police Department and Palm Beach County Sheriff's office report significant flooding near the intersection of Haverhill Road and Okeechobee Boulevard just NW of Palm Beach International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Established southwesterly flow, combined with plentiful low-level moisture, allowed for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to move towards the east coast metro in the afternoon. Heavy rainfall rates associated with these storms lead to flooding of streets across areas of Broward and Miami-Dade Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 3 inches of rainfall resulted in two of the three lanes of Sample Road in both directions between Powerline Road and NW 18th avenue are impassable from earlier heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level troughing over the eastern US helped keep abundant tropical moisture in place via southwesterly flow. Scattered to numerous afternoon thunderstorms produced gusty winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall that measured over 7 inches in about 3 hours across portions of Kendall, centered near the intersection of SW 117 Avenue and SW 120 Street. This rainfall produced flash flooding and resulted in water approaching and reaching the bases of various structures and warehouses. There were also multiple media reports of flooding with impassable roadways.
